summary_for_rag: Early Burgoyne paper from his Adelaide postgraduate period. Demonstrated that crystallisation of yeast cytochrome b2 (L-lactate dehydrogenase) requires prior conformational modification of the enzyme and partial DNA breakdown. Established that the crystalline enzyme is an artefactually modified form, not the native physiological species. Notable for early observation of DNA involvement in protein biochemistry, foreshadowing Burgoyne's later nucleic acid career.

summary_for_rag: Burgoyne's first lead-author paper. Proved that the DNA in crystalline cytochrome b2 is an artefactual degradation product of yeast genomic DNA, not a biologically significant component. Showed the enzyme selects DNA by size (~2e5 MW) and double-strandedness, independent of base sequence. Proposed as a model for virus assembly. Demonstrates early Burgoyne interest in DNA-protein interactions that would define his later career in chromatin biology.

Codifies the Adelaide group's work into the standard biochemistry reference series.

summary_for_rag: Final paper in Burgoyne's cytochrome b2 trilogy from his Adelaide PhD. Used electron microscopy to determine molecular dimensions (92x82x26 A) and crystal architecture of both DNA-free (hexagonal honeycomb tubes) and DNA-containing (alternating 90-degree protein layers with DNA in grooves) crystal forms. Structural model predicted DNA content matching observations. Demonstrates how nucleic acid fundamentally alters protein assembly architecture. PhD thesis work.

summary_for_rag: First purification and physical characterisation of the Neurospora arom multienzyme aggregate, carrying five sequential aromatic biosynthetic enzyme activities. Demonstrated MW of 231,000, composed of two identical halves. All five activities co-purified inseparably. Became a foundational reference for the arom system as a textbook example of multienzyme complexes. Burgoyne's proposal of identical dimer halves was prescient - later work showed fungal AROM is a single pentafunctional polypeptide. Most significant paper from Burgoyne's Neurospora period.

summary_for_rag: First paper from Burgoyne's Flinders University period, introducing the concept of a Ca2+-dependent endonuclease in mammalian nuclei. Showed that isolated rat liver nuclei have active DNA polymerase but cannot initiate synthesis on their own intact DNA. Ca2+ triggers endonucleolytic nicking, generating 3'-OH primer termini for DNA polymerase. Used araCTP chain terminator to quantify primer site production. This Ca2+-dependent endonuclease is the same activity that Hewish & Burgoyne (1973) later showed cuts chromatin at internucleosomal sites - making this paper the conceptual origin of the nucleosome discovery.

key_findings: Ca2+-dependent DNA synthesis confirmed in thymus nuclei (extending liver nuclei findings)

Authors note Ca2+-dependent nicking alone cannot account for replicon initiation specificity

summary_for_rag: Companion paper extending Ca2+-dependent DNA synthesis findings from liver to thymus nuclei. Connected the in vitro nuclear endonuclease observation to Whitfield's established in vivo work showing Ca2+ promotes thymocyte mitosis. Showed Ca2+ dose-response with half-maximal at ~30 uM free Ca2+. Ruled out cyclic AMP as mediator. Insightfully noted that Ca2+-dependent nicking alone cannot provide specificity for replicon initiation - the endonuclease would later prove more important for chromatin structure than replication control.

Drugs interfere with primer site effectiveness not direct polymerase inhibition

summary_for_rag: Mechanistic study using the resting rat liver nuclear system to show that ethidium bromide, proflavin and actinomycin D all inhibit DNA synthesis by interfering with primer site effectiveness rather than directly inhibiting DNA polymerase. Inhibition was progressive but reversible by adding nicked DNA. Ethidium bromide also inhibited nuclear exonuclease (path-clearing) activity. Warned that using these drugs for comparative nuclear/cytoplasmic studies gives misleading results.

araATP and araCTP compete successfully with normal dATP and dCTP respectively

summary_for_rag: Definitive full-length Biochemical Journal paper characterising the nuclear DNA synthesis system. Comprehensive nearest-neighbour analysis confirmed template-directed synthesis. Both araAMP and araCMP demonstrated as chain terminators that incorporate to a limit set by primer site availability, then stop until new sites are created. No difference between resting and regenerating liver nuclei. Authors honestly concluded the in vitro system is an imperfect reflection of in vivo processes. Notable for the death of co-author M. R. Atkinson (30 May 1970) during this work.

summary_for_rag: Collaborative paper between Adelaide (Surgery) and Flinders showing that rat lymphoid tissues (lymph nodes, thymus) depend on preformed purines from the liver rather than de novo synthesis. Portal occlusion, perfusion and in vitro experiments all showed selective depression of purine but not pyrimidine incorporation. Immunosuppressive drugs azathioprine and 9-butylazathioprine produced the same pattern, suggesting inhibition of purine supply contributes to their immunosuppressive action. Peripheral to Burgoyne's main research line but illustrates Adelaide-Flinders collaborative network.

Pulse-chase with excess cold dTTP confirmed true fragment processing, not artefactual degradation
Not Ca2+-dependent unlike isolated nuclei — indicating replication-type rather than repair-type synthesis
Direct nucleotide incorporation confirmed — not via conversion to thymidine
Labelled DNA was nuclear not mitochondrial (resistant to urea and Triton X-100 washes)
Removing cytoplasmic sheath caused persistent rather than transient low-MW DNA — environment sensitivity

summary_for_rag: Burgoyne's first sole-author paper (1972) demonstrated Okazaki fragment synthesis and processing in permealysed Ehrlich ascites tumour cells. Using Ficoll density gradients and homogenisation, he created a preparation retaining nuclear architecture while allowing direct entry of nucleotide triphosphates. Pulse-chase experiments confirmed that low-MW DNA fragments were genuine replication intermediates chased into high-MW DNA. Unlike isolated nuclei, this system was not Ca2+-stimulated, indicating replication-type rather than repair-type synthesis. The paper first cites D. R. Hewish (via Wallace et al. 1971) and notes Waqar's PhD completion at Flinders.

title: Chromatin Sub-Structure. The Digestion of Chromatin DNA at Regularly Spaced Sites by a Nuclear Deoxyribonuclease
key_findings: Ca-Mg endonuclease digests nuclear DNA in situ producing discrete bands on polyacrylamide gels — at least 6 bands forming a ladder
Molecular weights of fragments are integer multiples of the smallest unit — straight line on sqrt(mobility) vs log(MW) plot
Bands are genuine covalent fragments not concatenates — confirmed by re-electrophoresis of eluted bands
Same enzyme on naked DNA produces only a continuous smear — regularity comes from protein-DNA structure not enzyme specificity
Pancreatic DNase I on nuclei also produces only a smear — Ca-Mg endonuclease has unique chromatin specificity
Proposed chromatin has a simple basic repeating substructure with regularly spaced nuclease-accessible sites — essentially predicting the nucleosome
builds_on: Burgoyne, Waqar & Atkinson (1970a,b): Discovery of Ca2+-dependent endonuclease in mammalian nuclei
Hewish & Burgoyne (in preparation): Ca-Mg endonuclease characterisation

summary_for_rag: LANDMARK PAPER (~1172 citations). Hewish & Burgoyne (1973) provided the first experimental evidence for a repeating subunit structure in chromatin. Using the Ca-Mg endonuclease previously discovered in the Burgoyne lab, they showed that digestion of nuclear DNA in situ produces a discrete ladder of DNA fragments whose molecular weights are integer multiples of the smallest unit. Three controls proved this: (1) same enzyme on naked DNA gives only a smear; (2) DNase I on nuclei gives only a smear; (3) re-electrophoresis confirms genuine fragments. They proposed chromatin has a 'simple, basic, repeating substructure' — essentially predicting the nucleosome. This paper directly informed Kornberg's nucleosome model (1974), Olins & Olins' nu body visualisation (1974), and Noll's micrococcal nuclease confirmation (1974). Kornberg received the 2006 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for work on eukaryotic transcription that built on chromatin structure understanding originating with observations like this.

title: The Calcium Dependent Endonuclease Activity of Isolated Nuclear Preparations. Relationships Between Its Occurrence and the Occurrence of Other Classes of Enzymes Found in Nuclear Preparations
key_findings: Ca-Mg endonuclease has near-absolute requirement for both calcium and magnesium ions

MW ~40,000 with Mg2+ or ~20,000 without — suggesting Mg-dependent dimer
Activates DNA toward polymerase action — confirming connection to Ca2+-dependent priming
Increases ~6-fold during late liver regeneration (72h post-hepatectomy) — after peak DNA synthesis
Detected in mouse/rat liver, rat/rabbit/calf thymus; absent from rabbit bone marrow
Distinct Mg-only endonuclease in buffer D nuclei — virtually inactive on DNA in situ unlike Ca-Mg enzyme
Ionic environment controls which enzymes associate with nuclei

summary_for_rag: Companion paper to the landmark chromatin substructure paper, submitted same day (28 March 1973). Characterises the Ca-Mg endonuclease: requires both Ca2+ and Mg2+ (near-absolute), MW ~40,000 (Mg-dependent dimer, halves to ~20,000 without Mg), nuclear-localised, activates DNA for polymerase. Increases ~6-fold during late liver regeneration (72h) but after peak DNA synthesis — no obvious correlation with replication. Distinct from a separate Mg-only endonuclease in buffer D nuclei which cannot attack DNA in situ. Ionic environment controls nuclear enzyme complement — speculated to relate to cell cycle changes.

Two-case kinetic model unifies in vivo/in vitro discrepancies — Case I (few primers, fast chains) vs Case II (many primers, slow chains)
6% residual DNA synthesis is ara-CTP-resistant — qualitatively different process

summary_for_rag: Sole-author paper (1974) applying the permealysed Ehrlich ascites cell system to study the anti-cancer drug ara-CTP. Showed ara-CTP is a strong, rapidly reversible inhibitor of DNA synthesis — matching in vivo behaviour but differing from isolated polymerase systems. Proposed a two-case kinetic model: Case I (few primer sites, fast chain growth — like replication) gives rapid reversible inhibition; Case II (many primers, slow chains — like purified polymerase assays) gives slow progressive inhibition. This unified discrepant literature observations. 6% residual synthesis was ara-CTP-resistant. Demonstrates permealysed cells as a pharmacological tool for phosphorylated drug metabolites.

title: Mammalian Chromatin Substructure Studies with the Calcium-Magnesium Endonuclease and Two-Dimensional Polyacrylamide-Gel Electrophoresis
key_findings: Chromatin substructure regularity confirmed in mouse as well as rat — universal mammalian feature
Novel 2D electrophoresis: native PAGE then in-gel denaturation then denatured PAGE, using 7M urea to prevent renaturation
Each native DNA band n contains n classes of denatured DNA — integral relationship proves regular single-strand pattern
Double-strand fragment series is secondary consequence of regular single-strand nicking pattern
Higher-order bands are genuine DNA pieces, not hydrogen-bonded concatenates — 2D gel proved this definitively
Smallest double-stranded class MW ~120,000-150,000; smallest single-stranded class MW ~45,000-63,000 (approximately half)
Two levels of steric hindrance proposed: primary (regular repeat) and secondary (more random nucleoprotein shielding)

summary_for_rag: Follow-up to the 1973 landmark paper, extending chromatin substructure analysis to mouse (confirming it is a general mammalian feature) and developing a novel 2D polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis method to probe single-strand substructure within the DNA fragments. The 2D method separated native DNA in one dimension, denatured it in situ, then separated single-stranded DNA in the second dimension. Key finding: each native band of order n contained n classes of denatured DNA, proving that the double-stranded fragment series arose as a secondary consequence of regular single-strand nicking. Smallest ds fragment MW ~120,000-150,000; smallest ss fragment MW ~45,000-63,000. Ruled out concatenate artefacts. Proposed two levels of steric hindrance — primary regular repeat and secondary more random nucleoprotein shielding. 142 citations (OpenAlex).

title: The Reaction of the Ca-Mg Endonuclease with the A-Sites of Rat Nucleoprotein
key_findings: In standard 30-min digest 50-60% of potential single-stranded A-sites cleaved; only 0.5-1.5% DNA acid-soluble; 1.5-4% chromatin buffer-soluble
Cleavage distributed evenly throughout bulk genome — no gross clustering of susceptible sites
Single-strand breaks independent: Pds approximately equals (Pss)^2 — no cooperativity
Low-frequency cleavages occur between A-sites producing very fast-migrating fragments
Histones stable during digestion — no loss over 30 min; ratios constant through 60 min
Trypsin-resistant core retains A-site specificity — core histone-DNA structure defines accessibility
Micrococcal nuclease and Ca-Mg endonuclease attack the same A-sites — mixed digests give identical patterns
Mathematical model: proportion of DNA in band N = N(1-P)^(N-1)P^2 fits observed data

summary_for_rag: Quantitative study of Ca-Mg endonuclease action on rat liver chromatin A-sites (the regularly spaced inter-nucleosomal susceptible sites). In 30-min digests, 50-60% of single-stranded A-sites were cleaved while only 0.5-1.5% of DNA became acid-soluble. Cleavages were evenly distributed across the genome with no clustering. Single-strand breaks were statistically independent (Pds ≈ Pss^2). Histones remained stable throughout digestion. Trypsin digestion to a resistant core inhibited endonuclease activity but preserved A-site specificity — the core histone-DNA complex defines the cleavage pattern. Mixed digests with micrococcal nuclease confirmed both enzymes attack the same sites. First chromatin paper without Hewish; Mobbs promoted to co-author. ~30-60 citations estimated.

title: Chromatin Structure: A Property of the Higher Structure of Chromatin and the Time Course of Its Formation During Chromatin Replication
key_findings: Resting chromatin highly resistant to DNase I — only ~0.5% DNA acid-soluble over 90 min
Trypsin destroys DNase I resistance — ~20% acid-soluble by 90 min; removes H1 and higher-order packing
Calcium also destroys DNase I resistance — ~40% acid-soluble by 90 min
Micrococcal nuclease NOT hindered by superstructure — trypsin has virtually no effect on its action
Differential susceptibility suggests size-exclusion: blocks DNase I (41 Å) but not micrococcal nuclease (34 Å)
Newly synthesized chromatin transiently DNase I susceptible — as susceptible as trypsinised chromatin at 3.2 min post-pulse
DNase I susceptibility disappears within ~10 min — chromatin matures to fully resistant state
Three-stage maturation model: initial resistant phase (Weintraub) → susceptible phase (this paper) → final resistant state

summary_for_rag: Transition paper from nucleosome-level to chromatin higher-order structure. Demonstrated that resting chromatin is highly DNase I resistant (only 0.5% degraded in 90 min) but this resistance is destroyed by trypsin or calcium. In sharp contrast, micrococcal nuclease is unaffected by the superstructure. The differential susceptibility suggests size-exclusion packing: blocks DNase I (41 Å diameter) but not micrococcal nuclease (34 Å). Using intraportal 3H-thymidine pulse-labelling in regenerating rat liver, showed newly synthesized chromatin is transiently DNase I-susceptible (comparable to trypsinised chromatin at 3.2 min post-pulse) but matures to full resistance within ~10 min. Proposed three-stage maturation model. First appearance of A. J. Marshall as collaborator. ~40-70 citations estimated.

title: Interpretation of the Properties of Chromatin Extracts from Mammalian Nuclei
key_findings: Mg2+-stabilised nuclear preparations cause extensive chromatin degradation — DNA MW peak at 0.14-0.38 x 10^6 Daltons
Degradation during Mg2+ preparation is ~10x greater than controlled 3-min Ca-Mg endonuclease digest
Polyamine-stabilised nuclei (EDTA/EGTA) retain intact ultra-high MW DNA
Newly-synthesised DNA is degraded more slowly than older DNA during preparation — higher specific 3H radioactivity in larger fragments

summary_for_rag: Methodological warning paper demonstrating that standard Mg2+-stabilised nuclear preparations cause extensive and variable chromatin degradation via endogenous Ca-Mg endonuclease activity, even at 0-4°C. Degradation during a several-hour Mg2+ preparation was ~10x greater than a controlled 3-min deliberate digest. Importantly, newly-synthesised DNA was degraded more slowly than older DNA (higher specific 3H activity in larger fragments), explained by lower Ca-Mg endonuclease levels in dividing cells. This bias could explain Berezney & Coffey's observation of preferential retention of newly-synthesised DNA by nuclei as a simple artefact of differential fragment size. Exogenous micrococcal nuclease showed no such bias. Advocates for polyamine-stabilised preparations (EDTA/EGTA) to avoid these problems. Marshall as first author; companion to Burgoyne, Mobbs & Marshall (1976). ~20-40 citations estimated.

title: Unstable, Heterogeneous Physical States of Nuclear Bodies During the Post-Hepatectomy Dedifferentiation-Replication-Redifferentiation Sequence
key_findings: Unwashed nuclei show cyclic density shift during regeneration: decrease from ~2.4 M to ~2.3 M sucrose days 1-3 (S-phase/mitosis), then increase to ~2.45 M by day 8 (redifferentiation)
Day 3 nuclei show extreme density heterogeneity with no clear peak — asynchronous population in various cell cycle states
Density changes not observable in washed polyamine-stabilised nuclei — the density-altering components are stripped during purification
Confirmed true density equilibrium, not sedimentation rate effect (bottom-loading control)
Molecular redistribution experiments show nuclei both lose and gain macromolecules in fresh homogenates
Net density shifts reflect protein/lipid loss (depleted nuclei are denser; RNA loss cannot explain the effect)
Standard 2.1-2.4 M sucrose cushion purification selectively excludes lower-density nuclear types during regeneration
Density cycle begins after S-phase and during/after mitosis — possibly linked to nuclear protein matrix re-establishment

summary_for_rag: Third Marshall & Burgoyne collaboration showing that nuclei undergo cyclic changes in buoyant density during liver regeneration after partial hepatectomy. Unwashed nuclei (minimal purification, direct from homogenate) decreased in density from ~2.4 M to ~2.3 M sucrose during days 1-3 post-hepatectomy (coinciding with S-phase and mitosis), then increased to ~2.45 M by day 8 (redifferentiation). At day 3, nuclear population was maximally heterogeneous with no clear density peak. These changes were invisible in extensively purified (washed, polyamine-stabilised) nuclei which remained at ~2.5 M. This implies standard nuclear purification through 2.1-2.4 M sucrose cushions selectively loses lower-density nuclear types from dividing or recently-divided cells. Molecular redistribution experiments (dual 3H/14C label) showed nuclei both lose and gain macromolecules in fresh homogenates. Density shifts reflect protein/lipid loss. Published in Cell Differentiation, broadening the lab's audience beyond nucleic acids journals. ~15-30 citations estimated.

title: Chromatin Superstructure: The Next Level of Structure Above the Nucleosome Has an Alternating Character. A Two-Nucleosome Based Series Is Generated by Probes Armed with DNAase-I Acting on Isolated Nuclei
key_findings: DNase-I-armed probes (ferritin-DNase I, BSA-DNase I) generate a 2N-based (~400 bp) repeat from intact nuclei — every second nucleosome is resistant to attack by large probes
2N-based repeat observed in both chicken erythrocyte and rat liver nuclei — conserved feature
Free DNase I alone weakly produces the same 2N pattern — not a probe artefact
Series extends to at least 8N — alternating pattern spans stretches of 8+ nucleosomes
Four structural models proposed (Fig. 5): zigzag, sinusoidal, compact helical, coil-stacking — all compatible with Thoma/Koller/Klug EM zigzag
Novel reversible Ca-Mg endonuclease suppression using sodium dithiobisnitrobenzoate (DTNB) — reversed by thiol agents
Asymmetric 2N peaks after extended digestion suggest susceptible zones contain multiple cleavage sites

summary_for_rag: Burgoyne & Skinner used DNase-I attached to large protein carriers (ferritin, BSA via glutaraldehyde crosslinking) to probe intact nuclei. The large probes could only attack physically accessible nucleosomes, and the resulting single-strand DNA fragments (alkaline agarose gels) showed a ~400 bp (2N-based) repeat rather than the standard ~200 bp (1N) repeat. This indicates every second nucleosome in a series is shielded by its neighbours while the alternating ones project outward — establishing the dinucleosome as the basic unit of chromatin higher-order packing. Observed in both chicken erythrocyte and rat liver nuclei. Series extended to 8N. Compatible with Thoma/Koller/Klug zigzag model from EM. Also developed a novel reversible method for suppressing Ca-Mg endonuclease using DTNB. First US funding (Dept. of Health, Education and Welfare). First processed paper with J. D. Skinner, who becomes a long-term collaborator through the 1980s. ~30-60 citations estimated.

key_findings: 2N-based series extends to at least 10-12N in double-stranded fragments — no clearly defined end-point

Bound DNase I produces negligible 1N fragments; free DNase I produces significant 1N pieces — critical contrast
Near-absence of 1N from bound probes implies zigzag nucleofilament consistently folds to protect one side — evidence for structural rules above zigzag level
Sterically restricted 'core' at ~4N average fragment size (native) but not a single integral nucleosome multiple
DS:SS break ratio ~1:1 indicates strongly grouped/paired breaks rather than random
Low concentrations of free DNase I nearly as selective as bound probes — proposed DNase I binds endogenous actin increasing effective molecular diameter

summary_for_rag: Detailed follow-up to the 1981 BBRC dinucleosomal repeat discovery, using chicken erythrocyte nuclei. DNase-I-armed probes (ferritin, BSA carriers) generated 2N-based fragment series extending to 10-12N in double-stranded DNA. Each susceptible nucleosome has two attack sites ~70 bp apart (2N and 2N-70bp peaks), interpreted as the two DNA turns each having one exposed region. Critical finding: bound probes produce negligible mononucleosomal (1N) fragments while free DNase I produces significant amounts. This near-absence of 1N from bound probes implies the zigzag nucleofilament consistently folds to protect one side from attack — unilateral protection requiring definite structural rules. This is evidence for another level of structure above the zigzag. All digests approach a limit 'core' at ~4N average fragment size. Proposed that free DNase I gains structural selectivity by binding endogenous actin. Articulated the 'structural signature' concept: probe-generated fragments reflect probe-accessible inter-site distances. Published in Nucleic Acids Research. ~30-50 citations estimated.

H3 behaves as a 'non-core' histone in terms of exposure — rapidly digested like H1/H5
Increasing trypsin diameter 3-5x with polyacrylamide doesn't change order of events — initial attack sites in cavities >4-5 nm
Two classes of nuclear cavities confirmed: ~4-5 nm and ~10-15 nm — constrains solenoidal models
Structural collapse is abrupt: turbid suspension → clear solution with aggregated clump

summary_for_rag: Extended the Burgoyne lab's size-selective probe approach from nucleases to proteases. Compared free trypsin (4.2 nm) with trypsin conjugated to soluble polyacrylamide (PA-trypsin, ~10-30 nm) digesting chicken erythrocyte and rat liver nuclear histones. H1/H5 and H3 co-digest with concerted (type I) kinetics — intimately associated. Core histones (H2) have a definite lag and are not attacked until 50-60% of H3 is degraded. Critical finding: H3 degradation rate relative to structural collapse (%SC) is unchanged by enlarging trypsin with polyacrylamide, while H1/H5 rate drops ~50%. This indicates H3 destruction is the primary cause of chromatin superstructure collapse. H3 behaves as a 'non-core' histone despite being in the octamer. Initial tryptic attack sites are in cavities >4-5 nm in both nuclei types, constraining solenoidal chromatin models. New collaborator G.T.P. Saccone (first author). Published in FEBS Letters. ~15-30 citations estimated.

V-8 protease fingerprinting shows 37K protein and actin are structurally unrelated — not an actin derivative

AT-specific: observed in 3 of 4 AT cell lines (AT1ABR, AT3ABR, AT5ABR) but not in normal controls

Not due to serum deprivation or EBV viral origin

summary_for_rag: First report of density-dependent actin regulation specific to ataxia telangiectasia (AT) lymphoblastoid cells. When AT cells (EBV-transformed) are stepped from low to high density, actin (43K) levels decrease markedly while a structurally unrelated 37K protein increases by orders of magnitude. V-8 protease fingerprinting confirmed the two proteins are unrelated. Observed in 3 of 4 AT lines but not in normal controls. Not due to serum deprivation or EBV. Represents a significant shift from Burgoyne's chromatin work to disease cell biology, connected through the actin-DNase I interaction noted in his 1982 chromatin paper. Peter McKinnon (first author) later became a prominent AT/neurodegeneration researcher at St. Jude. Published in BBRC. ~20-40 citations estimated.

Constitutive expression in GM1525/GM1526 AT lines; 5 of 6 AT lines positive
No initiating signal found other than high cell density
Cytoskeletal hypothesis proposed for AT pathogenesis

summary_for_rag: McKinnon and Burgoyne (1985) demonstrate that the abundant 37K protein appearing in ataxia-telangiectasia lymphoblastoid cells is an actin-derived proteolysis product, correcting their 1984 conclusion that the two proteins were unrelated. Complete tryptic digestion of 125I-labelled proteins showed identical 2D peptide maps for actin and 37K, while the earlier V-8 partial proteolysis was misleading due to different intermediate cleavage paths. The 37K protein is the protease-resistant actin core produced by cleavage near the amino-terminal end, explaining the pI shift from 5.6 to 6.3, loss of antibody recognition, and identical iodinated peptide maps. Found in 5 of 6 AT lines (constitutive in GM1525/GM1526, density-dependent in others) but not in controls or Burkitt's lymphoma lines. No signal other than high cell density triggers the response. Authors propose a cytoskeletal hypothesis for AT pathogenesis linking actin dysfunction to radiation sensitivity, cancer proneness, and developmental abnormalities.

summary_for_rag: Drinkwater, Burgoyne and Skinner (1986) report two novel human repetitive DNA elements from a sperm library enriched in repetitive sequences. The 35s element is a 107 bp interspersed repeat found in the Factor IX gene intron-A (79% homology) with 250-500 copies per diploid genome, showing multiple unit-length variations. The 227s element is a satellite II tandem repeat with ~220 bp unit length and ~2500 copies, identified by TaqI site, degenerated ATTCC internal repeat, and characteristic EcoRI restriction ladder. This paper marks the transition from Burgoyne's chromatin structure programme to repetitive DNA genomics, launching a satellite DNA research programme that continued through the early 1990s. J.D. Skinner is identified by full name as Jennifer D. Skinner. Roger D. Drinkwater is a new first-author collaborator.

title: Chromatin structures: dissecting their mixed patterns in nuclease digests
key_findings: Four features in Fe-DNase I digests: 2N repeat, 1N repeat, random DNA, triple peak splitting
2N pattern associated with condensed/metaphase chromatin (back-to-back zigzag folding)
1N pattern associated with more exposed chromatin (face-to-face layering)
Random component is structural not artefactual - reflects Subirana face-to-face layering
Satellite III, ALU, and active DNA all show identical digestion spectra as bulk DNA
Systematic critique of all coil/solenoid models - none can explain extended disomal series
Back-to-back fibre model predicts 30nm width matching EM observations

summary_for_rag: Drinkwater, Wilson, Skinner and Burgoyne (1987) present the capstone paper of the Burgoyne lab chromatin superstructure programme. Using Fe-DNase I probes on human lymphoblast nuclei, they identify four distinct features in digestion patterns: a dinucleosomal (2N) repeat associated with condensed/metaphase chromatin (back-to-back zigzag folding), a mononucleosomal (1N) repeat from more exposed chromatin (face-to-face layering), a major random DNA component reflecting disorderly face-to-face layering, and triple splitting of peaks from nucleosome surface DNA exposure patterns. Cell-cycle analysis using mitotic blocking showed the 2N pattern specifically emerges in >95% mitotic cultures. Hybridisation with satellite III, ALU, and transcriptionally active DNA probes showed all have identical digestion spectra, ruling out distinct higher-order packaging for different sequence classes. The paper provides the most comprehensive published critique of solenoid and coil models, arguing none can explain extended disomal series, and reaffirms the back-to-back zigzag folding model as the adequate explanation.

title: The DNase I generated disomal series is coherent to 16N: Implications for coiling models of chromatin structure
key_findings: Disomal (2N) series coherent to 16N with no break in coherence or degeneration into 1N series
No coil signature (preferentially generated fragment class at coil cycle length) detected
Bridge-crossing coil models predict coherence break at ~6N — observed coherence to 16N eliminates these models
Klenow end-labelling with 32P-dATP and field-inversion gel electrophoresis enabled high-resolution large-fragment analysis
Coil models accommodating 16N coherence would require 14-16 nucleosomes per turn, exceeding 30 nm fibre diameter
Back-to-back double-stranded chromatin structures proposed as only compatible model

summary_for_rag: Davis and Burgoyne (1987) used Klenow end-labelling with 32P-dATP and field-inversion gel electrophoresis (FIGE) to follow the DNase I-generated disomal series from ferritin-DNase I probed chicken erythrocyte chromatin to its highest orders. The 2N series was coherent to 16N without any break or degeneration into a 1N series, and no 'coil signature' (preferentially generated fragment class at the coil cycle length) was detected. This result is incompatible with bridge-crossing coil models such as Williams et al. (1986) and Staynov (1983), which predict coherence loss at approximately 6N. Accommodating a 16N series within a coil model would require 14-16 nucleosomes per turn, producing diameters far exceeding the accepted 30 nm fibre width and requiring unreasonably long linker DNA. The authors argue this is evidence for the general unsuitability of coiling models and supports the existence of simple back-to-back double-stranded structures within chromatin, as proposed by the Burgoyne lab since 1981. This FEBS Letters communication is the companion paper to Drinkwater et al. 1987 NAR and together they represent the culmination of the Burgoyne lab dinucleosome programme.

summary_for_rag: Fowler, Drinkwater, Burgoyne and Skinner (1987) report that a probe (228S) derived from human satellite III DNA generates hypervariable restriction fragment length patterns upon TaqI digestion of human genomic DNA that are highly discriminating of individuals. The mechanism differs fundamentally from Jeffreys' minisatellite approach: rather than copy number variation detected by flanking restriction sites, satellite III hypervariation arises from random C-to-G point mutations within the degenerate TTCCA pentameric repeat, creating new TaqI (TCGA) recognition sites. CpG hypermutability at these sites may be contributory. The probe also identifies the 3.4 kb Y-specific fragment upon EcoRI digestion (Nakahori et al. 1986) and autosomal fragments, linking satellite III sequences to both sex-chromosome and autosomal biology. Germline/somatic tissue comparison confirmed constitutional inheritance of the variation. Centromeric tandem repeat reprobing served as invariant control for equal restriction. The complete 332 bp probe sequence was deposited (accession Y00139). This paper deepens the Burgoyne lab's transition from chromatin structure to satellite DNA and forensic DNA applications, representing the second paper in the satellite programme following the 1986 identification of satellite II repeats.

Whether the binding protein was PKC itself or a co-purifying contaminant could not be definitively established. The paper represents a collaborative side-branch bridging Burgoyne's DNA expertise with the Murray lab's PKC signalling programme.

Using in situ hybridisation and somatic cell hybrids, Fowler and Burgoyne showed that the TaqI-Deficient Polymorphic Sequences (TDPS) arise primarily from chromosomes 20, 21, and 22 rather than the expected K-domain chromosomes 9 and 15. The TDPS showed extreme sequence homogeneity, being resistant to multiple restriction enzymes, implying very recent TTCCA amplification events with minimal transposon invasion.

summary_for_rag: Foundational patent for FTA (Flinders Technology Associates) card technology. Sole inventor Leigh A. Burgoyne. Describes a cellulose-based filter paper impregnated with Tris (alkaline pH 8.0-9.5), EDTA (chelates Ca2+/Mg2+/Fe to prevent nuclease activity and free radical damage), SDS (denatures proteins, inactivates pathogens), and optionally uric acid (free-radical scavenger for long-term storage). Blood applied to the paper is protected from enzymatic, autolytic, and oxidative degradation. DNA can be extracted or used in situ for PCR. Polystyrene encasement enables archival storage with ~100% recovery after 36 months. Priority date October 5, 1988. The invention draws directly on Burgoyne's decades of understanding of DNA degradation mechanisms from his chromatin research. Became a global standard in forensic science, newborn screening, and biodiversity sampling.

summary_for_rag: Rogers and Burgoyne (1997) published the first peer-reviewed paper extending FTA card technology from blood DNA storage to bacterial applications. They demonstrated that bacteria (five Staphylococcus strains and one E. coli) applied to FTA paper are killed on contact, creating DNA-containing ghosts with cell walls rendered passive and completely penetrable to PCR reagents. No DNA extraction, lysozyme treatment, or purification was required. Using PCR ribotyping (16S-23S intergenic spacer amplification per Kostman et al.), they showed that both stringent phenolic and simple water washes gave equivalent ribotype patterns. The method worked across a broad template concentration window from undiluted to 1/1000 dilution (~3 pg DNA). S. aureus stored 1.6 years at room temperature on FTA still yielded good amplicons. Four distinct ribotype patterns from four random S. aureus clinical isolates demonstrated significant discriminatory power. All procedures were designed for automation compatibility, requiring only four simple wash operations with no two-phase extractions. The paper drew a conceptual link between bacterial autolysis on FTA and eukaryotic apoptosis/necrosis, foreshadowing Burgoyne 1999 pyknosis work. Craig Rogers appears as first author for the first time.

title: The Mechanisms of Pyknosis: Hypercondensation and Death
key_findings: Three distinct hypercondensation types distinguished in avian erythrocytes: anucleolytic pyknosis (starvation, no DNA breaks), nucleolytic pyknosis (exogenous nuclease, DNA fragmentation), and osmotic pseudopyknosis (gramicidin ionophore, external compression)

Pyknosis is conformational not passive: nuclei forced through decondensation-recondensation cycle remembered their original state (pyknotic or normal), proving molecular substructure determines condensation

High-precision electrospray mass spectrometry of all five major histones (H5 H3 H4 H2A H2B, errors <=1 Da) showed no modifications or proteolysis during pyknosis, eliminating histone modification as mechanism
Pyknosis occurs only in living cells undergoing slow metabolic decline; abrupt death by lysis does not cause pyknosis; once observable pyknosis is irreversible (14-20h starvation)
Standard apoptosis inducers (staurosporine, camptothecin, etoposide) had no effect, indicating a simpler signalling pathway than canonical apoptosis
Proposed histone tail-sharing entropy model: pyknosis driven by shift of histone tails from intranucleosomal to internucleosomal placement when rotational constraints between nucleosomes are relieved
Topoisomerase I cannot mediate pyknosis because full-integer 360-degree rotation does not change relative orientation of adjacent nucleosomes

summary_for_rag: Sole-author paper by L. A. Burgoyne in Experimental Cell Research (1999), 65 citations (OpenAlex). Using avian erythrocytes as a simplified model system, Burgoyne distinguished three mechanistically different forms of nuclear hypercondensation: (1) anucleolytic pyknosis triggered by glucose starvation with no DNA breakage, (2) nucleolytic pyknosis requiring exogenous nuclease with DNA fragmentation as in apoptosis, and (3) osmotic pseudopyknosis from gramicidin ionophore driven by external osmotic pressure. Forcible decondensation-recondensation experiments proved pyknosis is a conformational (remembered) state, not passive compression. Electrospray mass spectrometry of all five major histones at +/-1 Da accuracy showed no modifications during pyknosis, eliminating histone changes as the mechanism. Standard apoptosis inducers were ineffective. Burgoyne proposed a novel histone tail-sharing entropy model: when rotational constraints between nucleosomes are relieved, basic histone tails shift from intranucleosomal to internucleosomal placement, driving condensation through entropy of tail delocalisation. The most integrative conceptual paper of Burgoyne career, drawing together chromatin expertise from every prior phase.

summary_for_rag: Patent application publication (US 2001/0039010 A1) by sole inventor Leigh Alexander Burgoyne (Mitcham, Australia). Filed August 31, 1999 from provisional application 60/098,940 filed September 3, 1998. Published November 8, 2001. Addresses a critical practical gap in FTA card technology: the inability to visually confirm where colourless biological samples (saliva, urine, CSF) have been deposited on collection media. The invention incorporates an inert visible material - preferably bromophenol blue or carminic acid dye - printed in a pattern such as a grid, checkerboard, or dots onto the dry solid medium. When a liquid biological sample is applied, the dye dissolves and diffuses with the liquid, marking the wetted area against the undisturbed background pattern. This provides immediate visual confirmation of sample deposition for field quality assurance, biosafety (visible pathogen zones), and sample retrieval. Claims 15-16 integrate this visualization system with the core FTA protective composition including protein denaturing agent and free radical trap. Two method claims cover pre-applied dye diffusion and post-application staining. Alternative materials include metal sols, dyed latex particles, and pH-sensitive chromogenic indicators. 18 claims total. The indicating dye grid became a standard feature of commercial Whatman FTA cards.

They found 18 AMEL-Y negative individuals (3.2% of Indians, 0.6% of Malays, 0% of Chinese), all of whom also lacked DYS458, revealing a contiguous deletion of at least 1.13 Mb on Yp11.2 encompassing AMEL-Y, MSY1, and DYS458. Y-SNP haplogrouping showed 10 of 12 tested nulls belonged to haplogroup J2e, indicating a founder deletion event in an ancestral lineage from the Indian subcontinent. The study established that Amelogenin-based sex typing alone is insufficient for forensic casework in populations with South Asian ancestry.

title: Higher Failures of Amelogenin Sex Test in an Indian Population Group
authors: Yuet Meng Chang, Leigh A. Burgoyne, Katrin Both
summary: Systematic population study documenting amelogenin sex test failures in Malaysian ethnic groups. Found 3.6% failure rate in Indian males (4/112), 0.88% in Malays (1/113), and 0% in Chinese (0/113). Demonstrated casework failure in murder investigation where amelogenin Y-dropout would have caused male victim to be misidentified as female. Molecular characterization showed >1 Mb deletion on Y-chromosome short arm spanning both amelogenin and MSY1 minisatellite loci. Identified stable amelogenin-null/DYS438*9 haplotype in Indian males, suggesting ancient founder deletion. Validated Y-STR triplex (DYS438, DYS390, DYS439) as reliable backup for sex determination. All 338 population samples stored and amplified using FTA technology. Paper recommended incorporating Y-STR markers into routine forensic sex testing for populations with Indian ancestry.
significance: First systematic documentation of population-specific amelogenin failure rates showing 3.6% in Indian males is too high to ignore; first casework demonstration of amelogenin failure in murder investigation; identified stable Y-chromosome deletion haplotype in Indian populations; validated Y-STR backup testing; large-scale validation of FTA technology for population genetics.
connections: Builds on Chang, Both & Burgoyne (2002) Y-STR paper where amelogenin nulls first noted; uses FTA technology (Burgoyne 1994 patent); connects to subsequent 2007 Chang et al. amelogenin-negative males paper; corroborates Santos et al. (1998) and Thangaraj et al. (2002) findings of amelogenin deletions in Indian populations; influenced forensic laboratory protocols worldwide.

title: The Use of MSY1 Locus in the Investigation of Mixed Samples
summary: Development of semi-nested PCR method using junction-specific primers to type the highly polymorphic MSY1 Y-chromosome minisatellite locus for forensic mixture resolution. Modified flanking primer (Y1E+) eliminated non-polymorphic homolog interference. Three junction primer sets targeted type 1&3, type 3&1, and type 3&4 repeat junctions, generating length polymorphism patterns analyzable on standard forensic platforms. Validated on simulated mixtures, highly diverse mixed male DNA sample (500 sperm donors), and real gang rape casework where MSY1 typing resolved mixed male profiles when autosomal STRs showed complex degraded mixtures. Samples processed using FTA technology. Presented at 16th IAFS Meeting, Montpellier, 2002.

summary_for_rag: Transcript of BBC Radio Three programme Scientifically Speaking, broadcast 25 March 1974, presented by John Maddox. Francis Crick (1962 Nobel laureate, co-discoverer of DNA double helix) described the 1973 Hewish & Burgoyne chromatin substructure discovery as a "breakthrough" on national radio, explaining the quantised DNA ladder at multiples of ~200 base pairs to a broad audience. Crick credited Hewish and Burgoyne by name and noted the serendipitous nature of the finding. Morton Bradbury (Portsmouth Polytechnic) also discussed chromatin conformation. BBC cover letter dated 26 April 1974 addressed to Burgoyne at NIMR Mill Hill during his 1973-74 study leave with J. Tata. Transcript purchased for £3 + VAT. NOT a Burgoyne publication — external recognition by a Nobel laureate. The discovery Crick described provided key experimental evidence that influenced Kornberg's nucleosome model; Kornberg received the 2006 Nobel Prize in Chemistry. Burgoyne did not receive a Nobel Prize; significance is that his experimental work was foundational evidence for Nobel-recognised research.

title: DNA in the cell: chromatin
book_title: DNA in Forensic Science: Theory, Techniques and Applications
publisher: Ellis Horwood (Ellis Horwood Series in Forensic Science)
key_findings: Comprehensive review of chromatin structure (nucleosome through higher-order folding) written specifically for forensic scientists
Explains nucleosome as histone octamer (2x each H3, H2A, H2B, H4) wrapping ~140 bp DNA, with H1 linker extending to ~200 bp repeat
Three levels of chromatin folding above the nucleosome: zigzag nucleofilament, intermediate coiled/folded structure (citing Davis & Burgoyne 1987), and large-loop scaffold with Topoisomerase II

Sperm chromatin uses protamines instead of histones with disulphide cross-links providing extreme stability to detergents -- biochemical basis for forensic differential extraction of male vs female DNA in sexual assault cases
Detailed DNA extraction protocol for somatic cells: wash, detergent/protease, phenol, alcohol precipitation, with emphasis on preventing premature lysis and nuclease damage
Ca-Mg endonuclease (Burgoyne & Hewish 1978) produces diagnostic 200 bp ladder from nuclease-damaged somatic DNA; DNase I produces 80 bp and 10 bp periodicities
Sperm DNA degradation produces distinct pattern (~50 kb fragments then 72 bp periodicity) due to protamine-based chromatin structure
Quality problems catalogued: microbial contamination, mechanical shearing, UV-induced damage, transition-metal-catalysed free radical attack on deoxyribose
Less than 5% of genome transcriptionally active at any time -- active chromatin has less histone coverage and different protein associations

summary_for_rag: Chapter 2 of one of the first dedicated textbooks on forensic DNA science. Burgoyne, serving as both chapter author and co-editor of the entire volume, translates two decades of chromatin research into practical knowledge for forensic scientists. The chapter covers nucleosome structure, higher-order chromatin folding (citing his own Davis & Burgoyne 1987 model), sperm vs somatic chromatin differences (explaining the biochemical basis for differential extraction in sexual assault cases), detailed DNA extraction protocols, nuclease damage diagnostics (the Ca-Mg endonuclease 200 bp ladder from his 1970s discoveries), and quality problems including microbial contamination, UV damage, and transition-metal-catalysed radical attack. This is a KEY TRANSITIONAL DOCUMENT bridging Phase 3 (chromatin structure) and Phase 4 (forensic DNA): every fundamental discovery from the 1970s-1980s is here reframed as practical forensic knowledge. The degradation mechanisms catalogued map directly onto the FTA card patent design rationale (filed 1988, granted 1994). Co-editors Robertson (AFP) and Ross (Forensic Science Centre) represent the forensic establishment that Burgoyne was entering. The family copy bears the inscription 'To Mark from DAD' dated 28/10/2021.

burgoyne_role: Cited — Kornberg credits Hewish & Burgoyne with 'the first such observation' of the repeating chromatin subunit
summary_for_rag: Roger Kornberg's landmark 1974 Science paper proposing the nucleosome model — the repeating unit of eight histones and ~200 bp DNA that is the fundamental building block of chromatin. Kornberg explicitly cited Hewish & Burgoyne (1973) as providing 'the first such observation' of the ~200 bp repeating pattern, calling it a 'striking' convergence with his histone oligomer data. This paper, with ~2,606 citations, is the direct intellectual link between Burgoyne's 1973 discovery and the 2006 Nobel Prize in Chemistry. Kornberg was at MRC LMB Cambridge, the same institution as Crick, and thanked both Crick and Klug for discussions.

title: The Regular Substructure of Mammalian Nuclei and Nuclear Ca-Mg Endonuclease
book_title: The Cell Nucleus, Volume 4: Chromatin, Part A
key_findings: Authoritative review synthesising the entire Burgoyne-Hewish chromatin substructure programme (1970-1977) into a single narrative for the definitive Cell Nucleus book series
Classification system for five nuclear preparations ranked by decreasing complexity from permealysed cells to isolated polymerases
Comprehensive summary of Ca-Mg endonuclease properties: MW 30-40 kDa, requires both Ca2+ and Mg2+, double-strand selective, nuclear-localised, inhibited by polyribo-ADP
Detailed explanation of 2D gel electrophoresis proof that chromatin fragments are true integer multiples — ds class N yields N single-stranded classes — independent of MW determination
Statistical analysis of endonuclease attack: sites randomly intermingled (not clustered), second-strand cleavage independent of first (Pds ≈ Pss²)

Chromatin reprecipitation experiments: ghost-free soluble chromatin self-assembles into smooth spheres resembling nuclei; H1-free nucleoprotein forms viscous liquid droplets — intrinsic chromatin-chromatin forces may explain gross nuclear structure
Trypsin effects on Ca-Mg endonuclease: does not destroy regular pattern but subtly restricts the enzyme, causing digestion to stop earlier
Prescient speculation that Ca-Mg endonuclease's main natural function is programmed nuclear destruction during necrosis — foreshadowing apoptotic DNA laddering and Burgoyne's 1999 pyknosis paper

summary_for_rag: Invited review chapter for Harris Busch's authoritative Cell Nucleus series. Burgoyne and Hewish present the definitive account of their chromatin substructure programme, from the Ca2+-dependent DNA synthesis studies (1970) through the landmark 1973 discovery to the higher-order packing evidence (1975-1977). Key content: classification of nuclear preparations by complexity, comprehensive Ca-Mg endonuclease characterisation, detailed explanation of 1D and 2D gel electrophoresis proofs of regular chromatin substructure, statistical analysis showing random intermingling of susceptible and resistant sites, chromatin reprecipitation forming nuclei-like spheres without membranes, and comparison with micrococcal nuclease, DNase I, DNase II, and restriction endonucleases. The chapter ends with a prescient speculation that the Ca-Mg endonuclease functions in programmed nuclear destruction during necrosis — directly foreshadowing apoptotic DNA laddering and Burgoyne's own 1999 pyknosis paper. This is the last Burgoyne-Hewish co-authored publication, making it the valedictory document of their scientific partnership.
